# Feedback, Evidence, and Commitment {#sec-feedback-verification-trust}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"Program testing can be used to show the presence of bugs, but never to show their absence!"*
>
> — Edsger W. Dijkstra, *Notes on Structured Programming* (1970)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Edsger W. Dijkstra, a Turing Award-winning pioneer in algorithm design, highlighted the fundamental limitation of testing. For this chapter, the quote frames verification gates. Tests and checks do not prove an architecture claim complete; they give simulators, constraints, regressions, security screens, and reviewers authority to reject unsupported candidates before commitment.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Architecture 2.0 does not ask architects to trust an AI-assisted system. It asks them to control how a claim moves from feedback to scoped evidence and then to an authorized commitment. The design-loop card indexes one visible decision chain: claim, supporting evidence, counterevidence and failed checks, disposition (rejection, revision, escalation, or waiver), and accountable decision. Confidence, fluent explanation, and a stronger tool result do not advance commitment on their own.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What evidence supports an AI-assisted claim, what counterevidence can block it,
and who may authorize the resulting commitment?
:::

@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization treated methods as roles inside
a design loop. This chapter asks when their output may support a decision, and
it opens the card's evidence and decision fields: feedback budget, evidence,
failed runs and rejected alternatives, rejection checks and authority,
evidence-supported claim boundary, and accountable decision. An
Architecture 2.0 claim advances only when relevant feedback has become scoped
evidence, counterevidence and failed checks remain visible, the rejection path
can block the proposed transition, and a role with the required decision right
authorizes the commitment.

This distinction between generated output and verified evidence is central to the book. A model can generate a plausible
architecture description. A search method can find a strong proxy score. A
surrogate model can rank candidates. A tool-using agent[^fn-ml-tool-using-agent] can call simulators and
summarize results. None of those actions authorizes commitment by itself.
Assurance begins when the loop records what was measured, why it was relevant, how much
it cost, what assumptions it used, where the feedback is weak, which
alternatives failed, and what can say no.

[^fn-ml-tool-using-agent]: A tool-using agent is an AI system that can programmatically invoke external software tools or APIs, such as simulators or synthesis flows, to carry out a task. In this chapter its output counts as feedback rather than authorization, since the loop still has to record provenance and give an independent check the authority to reject what the agent reports.

The lighthouse prompt makes this need for evidence concrete. A proposed low-power 64-bit RISC-V
compute subsystem for XRBench under a 3\ W, 3\ nm-class low-power mobile envelope might look reasonable under an analytic proxy, promising under
simulation, and broken under synthesis or timing. It might meet performance
while missing an energy or thermal target. It might pass a benchmark but fail
a real deployment scenario. Architecture 2.0 therefore needs a commitment
discipline that is as explicit as its representations, environments, and
methods. At the center of that discipline is verification, used here more
broadly than its usual formal-methods sense.

> **Verification.** Verification is used broadly in this chapter. It includes
> formal methods when formal properties are available, but it also includes type
> checks, interface checks, regression tests, baseline replay, simulator
> cross-checks, synthesis constraints, physical-design warnings, security
> review, workload coverage, and expert design review. The common requirement is
> rejection capability. The check must be able to fail visibly rather than
> merely restate the method's output. As commitment risk rises, the loop also
> needs stronger independence from the method that produced the candidate.

Verification is therefore a family of checks that can exercise rejection
authority, not a single tool category (@tbl-verification-authorities). Inside AI-assisted loops, these checks are not generic quality checks; they
define which generated state transitions may continue, be revised, or be
blocked.

The familiar examples are prosaic for a reason. A parser rejects malformed IR,
a protocol checker rejects an illegal transaction, and a regression test blocks
a change that breaks expected behavior. Each is valuable because it can stop
the artifact from moving to the next state, not because it comments on quality.

| **Verification authority** | **Typical evidence** | **What it can reject** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Syntax and interface checks | Parsers, type checks, API checks, ISA or ABI (Application Binary Interface) conformance, and schema validation. | Artifacts that cannot legally enter the loop. |
| Functional checks | Unit tests, reference outputs, assertions, formal equivalence checking, and regression suites. | Candidates that compute the wrong behavior before performance matters. |
| Model and workload checks | Baseline replay, sensitivity studies, calibration, coverage, and drift tests. | Proxy wins outside the represented workload or calibrated support. |
| Implementation checks | Synthesis constraints, timing closure, power envelopes, area budgets, gate-level simulation (GLS), and design-rule checks (DRC). | Architecture candidates that violate physical limits or fail pre-silicon validation. |
| Operational checks | SLOs (Service Level Objectives), canary rollouts, rollback, telemetry, reliability, security, and incident review. | Claims that do not survive deployment conditions or policy boundaries. |
| Expert review | Design review, waived-warning review, risk acceptance, and commitment decision. | Results whose evidence is too weak for the proposed commitment. |

: **Verification names who or what can say no:** A credible claim should state
which independent authority can reject syntax, function, model support,
implementation feasibility, deployment behavior, or final commitment. {#tbl-verification-authorities tbl-colwidths="[21,36,33]"}

Inside an Architecture 2.0 loop, each check is bound to a represented state
field, a legal action, and a commitment boundary. It says which action may
continue, which state update is invalid, and when the loop must revise or
escalate.

The chapter works through one claim-to-decision chain (@tbl-trust-record-map),
deliberately treating commitment stakes before the rejection machinery that
protects them. The chain itself has five links, from claim to authorized
decision; the feedback-and-cost stage is not a sixth link but the resource
precondition that bounds what evidence the other stages can contain. The
design-loop card indexes the chain, while detailed cost, evidence, check, waiver,
and decision records support it.
Proxy mismatch and security boundaries test whether the chain remains credible
when metrics are gamed or evidence cannot be disclosed publicly.

| **Chain stage** | **Visible record** | **Reviewer question** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Claim** | Bounded task, workload, objectives, constraints, baseline, and proposed commitment. | What exactly is being asserted, and how far is it asking to go? |
| **Feedback and cost** | Available checks, observations, latency, cost, coverage, and fidelity. | What could the loop observe within its resources? |
| **Supporting evidence** | Claim-relevant observations with provenance, assumptions, uncertainty, and scope. | Which observations actually support the claim? |
| **Counterevidence and failed checks** | Failed runs, rejected alternatives, disagreements, missing coverage, and unresolved warnings. | What weakens or blocks the claim? |
| **Disposition** | Passed and failed conditions, rejection, revision, escalation, and any waiver. | Why did the claim stop, change, or continue? |
| **Authorized decision** | Commitment level, accountable decision right, consequences, and reopening conditions. | Who may authorize this transition, and what are they accepting? |

: **Assurance follows one visible chain:** A claim earns only the commitment
supported by scoped evidence after counterevidence, failed checks, rejection,
and waiver decisions remain visible.
{#tbl-trust-record-map tbl-colwidths="[22,37,31]"}

The design-loop card is the summary and index for this chain. Detailed
observations remain in supporting records, and a runnable receipt bundles the
card, inputs, runs, checks, and decision records when the loop claims
replayability. Card conformance, evidence fidelity, and commitment strength
remain separate claims. One does not advance automatically when another does.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can review the commitment chain for an AI-assisted loop
output.
That means you can:

- **Turn** feedback into scoped evidence through fidelity, provenance, uncertainty, and claim relevance;
- **Trace** a claim through supporting evidence, counterevidence, failed checks, disposition, and authorized decision;
- **Match** evidence requirements and decision rights to reversibility, blast radius, and consequences;
- **Inspect** a rejection path through its condition, executing check, independence basis, blocked transition, accountable owner, and waiver authority;
- **Explain** why architects must understand the loop even when tools execute routine checks.
:::

## Feedback Cost and Availability

@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces used feedback regimes to design
environments, and @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization used them to
choose method roles. This chapter uses the same economics to decide what
evidence the loop can obtain before commitment.

In any loop, the first assurance limit is economic. Feedback is not free, uniform, or
automatically useful. An architecture loop may have thousands of cheap proxy
evaluations, hundreds of simulations, tens of synthesis or physical-design
runs, a few emulation opportunities, and almost no chances to learn from
silicon or deployment mistakes. It may also have scarce human review time,
limited tool licenses, long queue delays, confidential workloads, and
organizational deadlines. @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales already measured
this limit. Verification staffing on IC/ASIC projects sits at rough parity
with design staffing, and projects spend roughly half their schedule in
verification, before any AI-generated candidate stream arrives
(@fig-ch07-verification-economics). Trusted disposition capacity is a
head-count, not a compute budget. These limits shape which methods are
appropriate and how much autonomy is acceptable.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch07-verification-economics
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Verification is already where the engineers went:** Across the Wilson Research Group / Siemens EDA functional-verification study waves, mean peak verification staffing on IC/ASIC projects rose from roughly 0.6 verification engineer per design engineer in 2007 to rough parity from 2012 onward, with processor projects reaching as much as 5:1, while projects spent roughly half their schedule in verification [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy]. The left panel reads the staffing ratio in levels; the head-count growth behind it is the scissors of @fig-verification-demand-scissors. The 2022 ratio derives from unlabeled study charts and is approximate; the 2022 time share is the study's reported 50-60 percent median. The rejection capacity this chapter budgets is scarce before a single AI-generated candidate arrives.
#| out-width: "86%"
#| fig-alt: "Two-panel plot of Wilson Research Group functional-verification study data. Left panel: verification-to-design engineer ratio on IC/ASIC projects rising from about 0.6 in 2007 to about 1.0 to 1.1 from 2012 through 2022, with a note that processor projects reach up to five to one. Right panel: share of project time spent in verification staying near 53 to 57 percent from 2014 through 2020, with a 50 to 60 percent median band for 2022."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter7-wilson-verification-economics.csv
# Wilson Research Group / Siemens EDA functional-verification studies (IC/ASIC),
# via Foster's published study summaries [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy].
# Left panel carries the verification-to-design staffing RATIO in levels,
# derived from the study's printed mean-peak staffing labels (2022 from
# chart-read bars constrained by the stated +50% design / +146% verification
# growth since 2007). The underlying head-count pairs are receipted with the
# ch02 scissors figure (chapter2-wilson-verification-staffing.csv); this
# chapter reads only their ratio. Time-share values 2014-2020 are study-figure
# annotations; 2022 is a stated 50-60% median.
ratio_series = [
    # (year, verification engineers per design engineer, approximate?)
    (2007, 0.615, False),
    (2012, 0.988, False),
    (2016, 1.105, False),
    (2020, 1.107, False),
    (2022, 1.009, True),
]
time_share = [(2014, 57), (2016, 54), (2018, 53), (2020, 56)]
time_2022_band = (50, 60)  # stated median range, 2022 wave

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(4.65, 1.95))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.105, right=0.985, top=0.86, bottom=0.185, wspace=0.42)

years = [s[0] for s in ratio_series]
ratios = [s[1] for s in ratio_series]
ax1.axhline(1.0, color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.8, zorder=0)
ax1.plot(years, ratios, color=COLORS["evidence"], linewidth=1.4, zorder=2)
solid = [(y, r) for y, r, approx_flag in ratio_series if not approx_flag]
approx = [(y, r) for y, r, approx_flag in ratio_series if approx_flag]
ax1.scatter([p[0] for p in solid], [p[1] for p in solid], s=16,
            color=COLORS["evidence"], zorder=3)
ax1.scatter([p[0] for p in approx], [p[1] for p in approx], s=18,
            facecolor="white", edgecolor=COLORS["evidence"], linewidth=1.0,
            zorder=3)
ax1.annotate("parity", (2007.4, 1.0), textcoords="offset points",
             xytext=(0, 3), fontsize=5.5, color=COLORS["muted"])
ax1.annotate("processor projects:\nup to 5:1", (2013.2, 0.68), fontsize=5.5,
             color=COLORS["evidence_ink"])
ax1.set_ylim(0.4, 1.35)
ax1.set_ylabel("verification / design\nengineers (mean peak)", fontsize=6.4)
ax1.set_xticks([2007, 2012, 2016, 2022])
ax1.set_title("Staffing ratio, IC/ASIC projects", fontsize=6.6, pad=4)

ax2.bar([t[0] for t in time_share], [t[1] for t in time_share], width=1.15,
        color=COLORS["blue"], zorder=2)
lo, hi = time_2022_band
ax2.bar([2022], [hi - lo], bottom=lo, width=1.15, facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"],
        edgecolor=COLORS["blue"], linewidth=0.8, zorder=2)
ax2.annotate("50-60%\n(median)", (2022, hi), textcoords="offset points",
             xytext=(0, 3), ha="center", fontsize=5.3, color=COLORS["workload_ink"])
ax2.set_ylim(0, 78)
ax2.set_ylabel("project time in\nverification (%)", fontsize=6.4)
ax2.set_xticks([2014, 2018, 2022])
ax2.set_title("Schedule share in verification", fontsize=6.6, pad=4)

for ax in (ax1, ax2):
    ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
    ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.55)
    for spine in ["top", "right"]:
        ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
    ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
    ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
```

The supporting cost record states which
evaluations, measurements, tool runs, human reviews, and deployment signals are
available, what they cost, how long they take, how reversible they are, and
what level of decision they can support. The record shows what kind of learning
and checking is possible. A Bayesian optimizer[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimizer-budget], reinforcement-learning policy[^fn-rl-policy],
surrogate model[^fn-ml-surrogate-proxy-feedback], critic[^fn-rl-critic], or tool-using agent should behave differently when a
feedback source takes milliseconds versus days, when a failed action is
reversible versus costly, and when the signal is a rough proxy versus a
signoff report (@tbl-feedback-budget-ledger).

[^fn-ml-bayesian-optimizer-budget]: The feedback-budget issue is how aggressively the optimizer may sample. A cheap proxy budget supports broad search; a costly synthesis budget demands selective acquisition and explicit evidence of each evaluation's fidelity.

[^fn-rl-policy]: In reinforcement learning, a policy is a function that maps an agent's observed state to a chosen action. Treated here as a method inside the loop, its output advances only through the evidence its actions leave and the checks that can reject them.

[^fn-ml-surrogate-proxy-feedback]: For this surrogate use, authority is the issue. A surrogate's ranking can guide exploration, but it should remain proxy feedback until an independent, higher-fidelity check can reject a proxy win.

[^fn-rl-critic]: In this book, a critic is the critique method role of @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization, a component that challenges assumptions, evidence, or claims inside the loop; reinforcement learning uses the same word more narrowly for a learned estimator of expected future returns. Its job here is to strengthen rejection, though it adds real authority only when it can fail in a way the generator cannot explain away.

| **Budget item** | **What to record** | **Why it matters** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Latency and cost** | Runtime, queue time, dollar cost, tool hours, license limits, model calls, GPU-hours, energy or carbon exposure, and human review time. | Determines whether the loop should search broadly, sample carefully, or mostly critique. |
| **Signal quality** | Fidelity level, metric definition, noise, determinism, coverage, and uncertainty. | Separates raw feedback from decision-grade evidence. |
| **Sample budget** | Number of possible runs at each fidelity, including failed runs and invalid candidates. | Forces sample-efficient methods and preserves failed runs and rejected alternatives. |
| **Reversibility** | Whether the action can be undone cheaply, re-run, patched, or rolled back. | Connects autonomy to risk. Reversible actions can tolerate weaker evidence than irreversible commitments. |
| **Decision impact** | Claim level supported, condition triggered, transition blocked, and accountable owner. | Ties each feedback source to the decision it can change. |
| **Scarce attention** | Expert review, debugging effort, validation bandwidth, security review, and integration time. | Prevents the loop from outsourcing cost to people whose time is the real bottleneck. |

: **Feedback budgets make learning economics explicit:** The supporting record states what feedback is available, what it costs, what evidence it can support, and when scarce expert attention or irreversible action should limit automation. {#tbl-feedback-budget-ledger tbl-colwidths="[22,34,32]"}

Beyond tracking costs, this record also changes what a result means. A method that finds a good point
after 10,000 cheap proxy evaluations has learned something different from a
method that selects three candidates for expensive synthesis. A loop that
records failures, timeouts, warnings, rejected candidates, and review notes
has more evidence than a loop that records only the winning score. This is
the connection to sample efficiency from @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization. Sample efficiency is not
only about using fewer evaluations. It is about making each evaluation carry
more architectural information.

To make that discipline concrete, the following notation acts as a review
prompt rather than a universal evidence metric. It makes the feedback budget
and sample-value question explicit:

$$
B = \sum_i n_i \cdot c_i,
\qquad
V_i \approx \frac{\Delta \mathrm{Conf}(d \mid e_i)}{c_i}.
$$

Here, $n_i$ is the number of evaluations of feedback type $i$, $c_i$ is
the cost of one such evaluation, $B$ is the total feedback budget, $e_i$
is the supporting evidence produced by that evaluation, $\Delta \mathrm{Conf}(d
\mid e_i)$ is the change in confidence for the decision $d$, and $V_i$ is the
resulting decision value of one such evaluation. It is the per-feedback-type
form of the sample-value rule from
@sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization; ordinal confidence changes or
decision-relevance labels are often more honest than unjustified numeric
confidence. The budget's shape is what makes the rule bite. As one evaluation's cost
climbs from milliseconds at the proxy rung to months at the silicon rung, the
affordable sample count collapses toward one (@fig-ch07-feedback-funnel).

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch07-feedback-funnel
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **The feedback funnel sets the budget the notation formalizes:** As the cost of one evaluation rises from milliseconds for an analytic proxy to months for silicon or deployment evidence, the affordable sample count $n_i$ under a fixed budget $B = \sum_i n_i \cdot c_i$ collapses from thousands toward one. Ranges are illustrative orders of magnitude from this chapter's funnel; the cycle-level rung is anchored to the wall-clock arithmetic of @sec-data-representations-world-models (one second of a 1 GHz target at 100 kcycle/s to 10 Mcycle/s of simulator throughput).
#| out-width: "86%"
#| fig-alt: "Log-log plot with cost per evaluation in seconds on the horizontal axis spanning milliseconds to months, and evaluations per study on the vertical axis spanning one to one hundred thousand. Five labeled boxes descend from analytic proxy at thousands to a hundred thousand cheap evaluations, through cycle-level simulation in the hundreds, synthesis and physical design in the tens, emulation at a few, down to silicon or deployment at about one."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Illustrative orders of magnitude from this chapter's funnel sentence
# ("thousands of cheap proxy evaluations, hundreds of simulations, tens of
# synthesis or physical-design runs, a few emulation opportunities, and almost
# no chances to learn from silicon"). The cycle-level cost span replicates
# ch04's wall-clock computation T_wall = f_target * T_workload / R_sim for a
# 1 GHz target running a 1 s workload at the mid-range illustrative
# throughputs of its simulation-time table (100 kcycle/s and 10 Mcycle/s).
f_target, t_workload = 1e9, 1.0
sim_cost = (f_target * t_workload / 1e7, f_target * t_workload / 1e5)

rungs = [
    # (label, cost range in seconds, evaluations range, color key)
    ("Analytic proxy", (1e-3, 1e0), (1e3, 1e5), "blue"),
    ("Cycle-level simulation", sim_cost, (1e2, 1e3), "purple"),
    ("Synthesis / physical design", (1e4, 3e5), (1e1, 1e2), "orange"),
    ("Emulation", (1e5, 1e6), (2, 8), "red"),
    ("Silicon / deployment", (3e6, 3e7), (1, 2), "magenta"),
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.65, 2.3))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.115, right=0.97, top=0.95, bottom=0.19)
ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_yscale("log")

label_offsets = {
    "Analytic proxy": (0, 8),
    "Cycle-level simulation": (0, 8),
    "Synthesis / physical design": (0, 8),
    "Emulation": (-6, 8),
    "Silicon / deployment": (0, 8),
}
for label, (c_lo, c_hi), (n_lo, n_hi), key in rungs:
    ax.fill_betweenx([n_lo, n_hi], c_lo, c_hi, facecolor=COLORS[key],
                     alpha=0.22, edgecolor=COLORS[key], linewidth=0.9, zorder=2)
    cx = (c_lo * c_hi) ** 0.5
    dx, dy = label_offsets[label]
    ax.annotate(label, (cx, n_hi), textcoords="offset points", xytext=(dx, dy),
                ha="center", fontsize=5.7, fontweight="bold",
                color=COLORS.get(key + "_ink", COLORS[key]))

ax.set_xlim(3e-4, 3e8)
ax.set_ylim(0.5, 4e5)
ax.set_xticks([1e-3, 1e0, 60, 3600, 86400, 2.6e6])
ax.set_xticklabels(["1 ms", "1 s", "1 min", "1 h", "1 day", "1 month"])
ax.set_xlabel("cost per evaluation (log scale)", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel("evaluations per study (log scale)", fontsize=7)
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(True, which="major", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.5)
for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
```

Architecture 2.0 weighs confidence gains against computational and human costs, much as the quantitative tradition weighed performance against area and power. For example, spending 100 CPU-hours ($c_i$) on a cycle-level simulation to gain a modest reduction in power, performance, and area (PPA) uncertainty ($\Delta \mathrm{Conf}$) might be less valuable than spending 1 hour of expert review to catch a fundamental security flaw. The notation is a question the loop designer must answer before
each stage. Will another simulation, synthesis run, expert review, or
deployment experiment change a decision enough to justify its cost? @sec-running-the-loop
makes the question concrete, spending the proxy budget freely, spending the
simulation-stage budget carefully, and deferring the high-fidelity power check
to the surviving candidate's next evidence stage.
The unit being priced is not just another evaluation. It is one part of the
claim-to-decision chain, including the state before the action, the action
itself, the observation it produces, any failed condition, the transition that
can be blocked, and the resulting decision.

## Fidelity Ladders and Supporting Evidence {#sec-fidelity-ladders-and-evidence-ledgers}

Feedback becomes evidence only when it is tied to fidelity, provenance,
uncertainty, and a decision. A simulator result is feedback. It becomes
evidence when the workload, simulator version, configuration, random seed,
assumptions, metric definition, failure status, and acceptance criterion are
recorded. A synthesis report is feedback. It becomes evidence when the
technology assumptions, constraints, tool versions, warnings, and comparison
baseline are explicit.

> **Fidelity ladder.** A fidelity ladder is an ordered set of feedback regimes
> used to test the same claim. Each rung names the observation source, cost,
> assumptions, coverage, uncertainty, and claim scope it can support. A higher
> rung may expose more implementation or deployment reality, but it does not
> automatically make the claim true or authorize a stronger commitment.

This way of connecting claims to evidence is not new to engineering.
Safety-critical fields formalize it as an *assurance case*, a structured
argument linking a top-level claim to sub-claims, assumptions, supporting
evidence, and defeaters. Goal Structuring Notation[^fn-safety-gsn] makes those
links explicit and reviewable. Architecture 2.0 uses a lighter claim-to-decision
chain, but it borrows the same discipline. The supporting evidence record shows
which observations support the claim, which failed checks or rejected
alternatives weaken it, and which waivers or decision rights allowed it to
continue. Naming the lineage matters because assurance cases already expose
common failures such as unstated assumptions, evidence outside the claimed
scope, and confidence that outruns support.

[^fn-safety-gsn]: Goal Structuring Notation (GSN) is a graphical argumentation notation used in safety-critical engineering to document the elements of an assurance case, making each inference and its supporting evidence explicit and reviewable [@Kelly2004GSN]. The claim-to-decision chain borrows this discipline so a design claim carries its support, assumptions, counterevidence, and decision rather than a bare score.

When a claim needs staged feedback, an Architecture 2.0 study should move it
through increasingly costly feedback sources, recording a scoped observation,
supporting or counterevidence, and disposition at each stage
(@fig-evidence-chain-rejection-gates).

![**The evidence chain connects an architectural claim to a decision:** Staged checks record provenance, uncertainty, counterevidence, and failed conditions while declaring which transition each check may block. The final disposition belongs to an authorized review role. Fidelity, cost, rejection, and commitment remain separate judgments; moving to a stronger check does not authorize a stronger commitment by itself.](images/F9-evidence-chain-rejection-gates){#fig-evidence-chain-rejection-gates width="100%" fig-alt="Staged architectural-claim-to-decision chain showing feedback sources, provenance, uncertainty, failed conditions, revision, rejection, escalation, and authorized review, with fidelity and cost kept separate from commitment authority."}

In the figure, a rejection gate hangs off every intermediate stage, the
evidence record spans the full chain beneath them, and the commitment decision
waits behind an authorized review at the end. Assurance is therefore a
property of the decision chain rather than the model or a single score.

However, moving up the fidelity ladder is not a simple ranking from false to true. Higher fidelity is not
automatically truth if the wrong workload, objective, constraints, or baseline
were used. A detailed physical-design result can still answer the wrong
architecture question. A deployment signal can still be confounded by a
software change. A benchmark can still be too narrow. The purpose of the
feedback-regime view is therefore not to worship expensive tools. It is to make
the path from weak feedback to stronger evidence explicit.

Returning to the lighthouse prompt, low-fidelity feedback may be useful for eliminating
obviously infeasible vector widths, memory sizes, or accelerator partitions.
Simulation may then test workload behavior and data movement. Synthesis or
emulation may expose timing, area, or power problems. Deployment-like traces
or silicon evidence may reveal workload drift or integration effects. At each
stage, the loop should ask whether the earlier conclusion survived, changed,
or should be rejected.

This framing is consistent with the quantitative tradition in computer
architecture, where measurement, abstraction, and careful comparison are
central [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. Architecture 2.0
adds a loop-level requirement. The supporting evidence record must be
inspectable by the compound method system and every role accountable for the
decision.

## Commitment Levels and Reversibility {#sec-commitment-levels-and-reversibility}

The supporting evidence record shows how strong the support for a claim is. How
strong that support needs to be is a separate question, and assurance requirements should rise
with commitment. A loop that generates a
candidate simulator configuration can tolerate more automation than a loop
that changes RTL, partitions a chiplet boundary (the physical interface between separate silicon dies), selects a package interface,
or recommends a deployment policy. The difference is not whether AI is
involved. The difference is rollback cost, blast radius (how far a wrong change
can propagate), and who bears the
consequence when the loop is wrong.

The exact
ordering will vary across organizations, but the pattern is stable across the
five commitment levels of @tbl-commitment-ladder, where each row pairs a
level's example actions with the discipline and the decision rights it
requires. Reversible exploration can use lighter evidence, while irreversible or
high-blast-radius decisions require stronger evidence, independent rejection,
and visible accountable decision rights. An escalation threshold states when
the loop must stop relying on its current feedback source and seek stronger
evidence, independent review, or the approval right required for the proposed
commitment.

Escalation thresholds depend on consequences, not only on model confidence, so
the decision rights behind them must be visible. A proxy win may be enough to keep
exploring. It is not enough to change a subsystem interface, waive a
verification concern, or commit to a power claim. The loop should therefore state in advance which
events force escalation, such as uncertainty outside the calibrated range, a benchmark
coverage gap, a failed tool check, a security boundary, a high rollback cost,
or a decision that would affect another team or product.

Architects do not need to execute every routine check. They do need to
understand the chain because architecture work sets the claim scope, decides
what may be delegated, judges evidence sufficiency, assigns waiver rights, and
accepts system consequences. An organization may distribute those rights across
design, verification, security, product, or release roles. The card must make
that distribution visible rather than treating every gate as a singular human
decision or as an autonomous tool choice.

| **Commitment** | **Example actions** | **Required discipline** | **Decision rights** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Exploratory | Generate hypotheses, configs, candidate questions, or design cards. | Basic validity checks and provenance. | Automated assistance within declared scope. |
| Experimental | Run simulator sweeps, tune compiler flags, select candidates for deeper study. | Workload versions, seeds, baseline, rejected candidates, and uncertainty. | Automated execution with a named review right. |
| Implementation | Change RTL, generators, tool constraints, tests, or runtime interfaces. | Tool checks, regression tests, synthesis or integration evidence. | Routine checks may be automated; an integration role owns advancement. |
| Integration | Change subsystem interfaces, chiplet boundaries, memory contracts, or product-facing policies. | Cross-tool checks, compatibility, security, and explicit escalation. | Named cross-team approval and waiver rights. |
| Irreversible | Mask-level choices, committed signoff decisions, fleet-wide rollouts, or customer-visible deployments. | Independent supporting evidence, inspectable rejection path, audit trail, and accountable decision. | Explicit commitment and waiver authority. |

: **Commitment level should govern decision rights:** Reversible exploration can tolerate lighter evidence, while interface changes, product policies, signoff decisions, and deployments require stronger evidence, independent rejection, and visible accountable ownership. {#tbl-commitment-ladder tbl-colwidths="[20,28,24,18]"}

This commitment structure keeps Architecture 2.0 from making a naive autonomy argument.
Autonomy is not a virtue by itself. A loop may be highly automated for
low-commitment exploration and deliberately conservative for high-commitment
decisions. In fact, the most valuable near-term systems may make no final design
choices at all. They narrow a space, surface contradictions, preserve
evidence, and leave the accountable roles better prepared to decide.

::: {#pri-escalate-commitment .callout-design-principle title="Escalate with commitment"}
In an AI-assisted loop, evidence requirements should rise with rollback cost,
blast radius, and the independence of the rejection path. The cheaper a decision
is to undo, the more routine work may be delegated. The harder it is to undo,
the stronger the evidence, check independence, and accountable approval must be.
:::

## Rejection Authority

@sec-architecture-20-ontology defined rejection authority as the decision right
to block a candidate or claim. This chapter deepens that definition by asking
what a reviewer can inspect when the right is exercised. The executing check may
be a parser, simulator, formal tool, regression test, synthesis flow, cross-tool
comparison, signoff rule, deployment signal, security policy, benchmark rule,
or expert review. Its rejection condition may expose a proxy mismatch, protocol
violation, physical limit, or policy boundary.

Beyond preventing errors, rejection authority is one lever of adjudication
capacity. In a rejection-bound stage with a fixed proposal stream, validated
cheap checks can settle some cases before expensive escalation. Total capacity
also depends on routing, audits, reference-check cost, and concurrency;
@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack turns these levers into a measurable capacity
model. A search
loop has another lever because a better generator may need fewer evaluations to
reach a fixed-quality target. Rejection authority is therefore a working part
of the loop, not a bolted-on safeguard, and it is one determinant of useful
design progress among several, not the whole story.

When a reviewer inspects an exercised rejection, the disposition stage of the
chain should expose six properties, from the exact condition that failed to
the distinct waiver right that may override it
(@tbl-rejection-review-properties).

| **Review property** | **What must be visible** |
| --- | --- |
| **Rejection condition** | The exact observation or missing requirement that causes failure. |
| **Executing check** | The tool, test, policy, review process, or deployment signal that evaluates the condition. |
| **Independence basis** | What the check does not share with the generator, such as model family, training data, objective, tool path, workload, or evidence source. |
| **Transition it can block** | The state change or commitment level that cannot proceed after failure. |
| **Accountable owner** | The role responsible for the check's operation, interpretation, and consequences. |
| **Waiver authority** | The distinct role or process allowed to override the failure, with rationale, scope, expiration, and reopening conditions. |

: **A rejection path must be inspectable:** Naming a check is insufficient unless
the condition, independence, blocked transition, ownership, and waiver right are
also visible. {#tbl-rejection-review-properties tbl-colwidths="[25,65]"}

An invalid constraint, timing miss, benchmark violation, or expert objection can
expose a design-space boundary. A simulator crash or failed build is recorded
first as an environment failure and becomes a candidate rejection only when a
declared validity or constraint rule makes the candidate responsible for that
outcome.

We can formalize this commitment discipline compactly as a boolean gate:
$$
\mathrm{Commit}_k(x) =
\begin{cases}
1, & \mathrm{valid}(x) \land E_k(x) \ge \tau_k \land
\rho_k(x) \le \rho_{\max,k}, \\
0, & \text{otherwise}.
\end{cases}
$$
This notation does not authorize a decision. It makes the refusal conditions
visible. Here, $x$ is the generated architecture candidate and $k$ is the
current commitment level (e.g., experimental vs. integration). For commitment
to be authorized at level $k$, three conditions must hold:

1. $\mathrm{valid}(x)$: Legality alone. The candidate breaks no declared ISA, structural, or interface contracts.
2. $E_k(x) \ge \tau_k$: Evidence sufficiency. The collected evidence $E_k(x)$ meets or exceeds the required threshold $\tau_k$ for that commitment level.
3. $\rho_k(x) \le \rho_{\max,k}$: Residual risk. The remaining uncertainty $\rho_k(x)$ is below the maximum risk $\rho_{\max,k}$ the organization is willing to tolerate.

Evidence and risk are written as scalars only for compactness. In practice each
is a set of per-level requirements in @tbl-commitment-ladder. The thresholds are policy
and judgment choices, not magic constants learned by the method. If validity,
evidence, or residual risk fails, the loop should reject, revise, or escalate
instead of silently turning output into commitment. @sec-running-the-loop runs this rule on
the lighthouse prompt and shows how a candidate can fail on validity, evidence,
or residual risk even when another metric looks promising.

The commitment ladder (@tbl-commitment-ladder) turns $\tau_k$ from a symbol into
a rule the loop can enforce. Read each level's required discipline as its
acceptance condition, and make the failure explicit. When a required item is
missing, the loop does not commit at that level. It either downgrades the
claim to the highest level whose evidence is actually present, escalates to
the feedback the missing item names, or rejects the candidate. An exploratory
claim missing a baseline stays exploratory; an implementation claim missing
synthesis evidence or an independent rejector drops back to experimental until
that evidence exists; an irreversible claim missing its accountable approval right
is not made. That is the whole point of writing $\tau_k$ down per level. A missing cell forces rejection or escalation, never silent commitment. And when a claim is
meant to be comparable across teams rather than only reviewable, these
thresholds cannot stay local judgment calls; a shared benchmark protocol has to
fix them, or the claim is contrastable (a reviewer can check it against its own
declared rules) but not comparable (two teams' claims cannot be ranked).

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="Validity is a contract, not a score"}
**Context.** The commitment rule above does not ask whether an AI-generated candidate sounds
promising. It asks whether the candidate is legal for the commitment level the
loop proposes to authorize.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** A generative method's candidate for the "64-bit RISC-V-based compute
subsystem" is legal only if it stays inside the declared ISA/software contract
and does not hide an invalid memory or SoC interface
assumption. A legal candidate can still be rejected when the evidence shows that
it misses the XRBench-class workload obligation or the 3\ W TDP target.

**Rejection gate.** ISA compatibility, workload validity, memory/interface
legality, power, deadline, and evidence sufficiency for the proposed decision are gates, not advisory
metrics for the generator to optimize.

**Takeaway.** If any gate is unsupported, the loop should force the generative method to reject, revise, or
escalate rather than turn a plausible subsystem into a commitment.
:::

Once a check fails, the response to rejection should be explicit. A candidate
is discarded. A representation gains a field. An environment gains a validity
check. A method loses action space. A workload gets a sharper coverage
definition. A claim is weakened. An accountable decision role escalates the
case. Without this response path, rejection becomes a
log message rather than a learning signal.

This rejection authority also protects against polished but unsupported outputs.
Tool-using agents can generate convincing summaries, plots, design reports,
and review notes. Those artifacts are useful only if the loop can still reject
them. In architecture, a beautiful explanation cannot overrule a failed
timing check, an invalid workload, a missing baseline, or a security boundary.
A deployed version of this independence appears at fleet scale in
@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack. During an instruction-set migration, build,
test, sanitizers, and a production monitor evict regressions automatically,
regardless of how confident the automated repair tool was.

The independence requirement grows sharper as verification itself becomes
AI-assisted. Machine-learning-assisted verification tools, such as Cadence
Verisium for triaging failures, ranking likely root causes, and directing
coverage, can be valuable [@Cadence2022Verisium]. But they move part of the
rejection authority into a learned system, which forces a recursive version of
this chapter's question. Is the authority that can reject a design independent
of the system that produced it, or has the loop quietly made the generator and
its judge the same model? AI-assisted verification can share failure modes with
generation, so a rejection authority that shares the generator's
blind spots is not an independent gate. It is a second opinion from the same
witness.

The same rule applies when rejection is split across components. A generator,
critic, verifier, and summarizer may be separate components in an implementation,
but they do not become independent merely by having different prompts or names.
Independence depends on what they share, such as training data, model family, objective,
tool path, evidence source, or human instruction. Software fault tolerance
learned this empirically. Knight and Leveson's multiversion-programming
experiment found that independently developed program versions fail on
correlated inputs far more often than an independence assumption predicts
[@KnightLeveson1986Multiversion]. A loop that asks several
correlated components to agree has created consensus, not rejection authority,
unless some gate can fail in a way the generator cannot simply explain away.

## Proxy Mismatch, Metric Gaming, and Calibration {#sec-proxy-mismatch-metric-gaming-and-calibration}

The failure an independent rejection authority most often has to catch is proxy mismatch, introduced in @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces. IPC may
improve while energy, area, or tail latency worsens. A simulator metric may
improve while synthesis exposes timing or power problems. A benchmark result
may improve while the real workload distribution changes. A Pareto frontier
may look convincing because all points were evaluated under the same flawed
proxy. An automated optimizer may appear capable because it overfits the evaluation loop,
not because it understands the architecture problem. In the machine-learning literature this active exploitation of a flawed metric is reward hacking (@sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces).

Two distinct failure modes hide within this proxy mismatch, and they need different cures. First, a proxy can simply be
miscalibrated or too narrow, mis-estimating the objective even when nothing is
pushing on it; a better-fitted or wider-coverage proxy helps. Second, a method can
engage in reward hacking, driving the design toward exactly what the proxy fails to
penalize (e.g., maximizing IPC by silently sacrificing thermal limits). If an agent discovers that moving a compute block reduces wire delay on a proxy model but ignores thermal hotspots, only an independent thermal constraint check can reject the layout before it becomes an expensive physical-design failure. Here, a better proxy is not enough; only an independent rejection
authority can catch the spurious win.

This is not a new problem created by AI. Benchmarks, simulators, cost models,
and design rules have always been approximations. What changes in Architecture
2.0 is the speed and persistence with which a method can exploit the
approximation. A human may notice that a score is improving for the wrong
reason. A search method may happily continue. A compound system may even
produce a persuasive narrative for a proxy win unless the loop asks for
calibration and counterevidence.

::: {.callout-war-story title="A proxy under dispute"}
**The claim.** AlphaChip (a reinforcement-learning method for macro placement) is one of the field's clearest recent disputes over whether a cheap proxy rewarded the wrong thing. Its loop optimized a fast proxy cost, a stand-in for the metrics that actually decide a chip.

**The gap.** An independent 2023 reassessment found that proxy had weak correlation with the post-route chip-quality metrics that decide a real chip, and little useful correlation with timing [@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL]. A candidate could look excellent on the proxy the loop used and only ordinary once a full flow charged what the proxy ignored.

**The lesson.** For this chapter, the useful part of the dispute is proxy mismatch at full-flow scale. A proxy result remains scoped feedback until the decision chain records its calibrated support and a stronger check can overturn it. An unverified proxy win is a reason to seek more evidence, never a reason to authorize commitment. The broader dispute also involved pretraining, compute, implementation, and test case choices, but the proxy question shows why evidence must mean what everyone assumes before the loop can ask for commitment.
:::

To prevent this kind of vanishing win, calibration acts as an escalation contract. It defines where a generative method may
use a proxy to rank candidates, where it must escalate, and what stronger
evidence can reject a proxy win. Prediction models used in
architecture have long needed validation against held-out data[^fn-ml-held-out-data], higher-fidelity
measurements, or carefully designed experiments
[@LeeBrooks2006RegressionModeling; @IpekEtAl2006PredictiveDSE]. The same
principle applies to AI-assisted loops. If a loop claims a candidate satisfies the
3\ W lighthouse envelope, the evidence must show how the power estimate was
calibrated, what workload region it covers, what uncertainty remains, and what
higher-fidelity result could overturn the claim.

[^fn-ml-held-out-data]: Held-out data is a subset of a dataset reserved during training and used only to test whether a model generalizes to unseen inputs. Here it is one way a proxy establishes calibrated support, since a prediction validated against held-out cases carries more evidence than one fitted and reported on the same data.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Calibration Gate Check"}
Does the loop know when a generator's proxy has left calibrated support? A proxy win supports a claim only when the candidate falls within that region. If it falls outside, the loop must seek a higher-fidelity check rather than treat the prediction as evidence.
:::

Benchmark rules are part of the
loop's evidence contract. If those rules are hidden or stale, automated optimizers can
optimize version quirks, protocol gaps, or unowned metrics while appearing to
make progress. As @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales and
@sec-loop-patterns-across-stack show, a benchmark becomes community
infrastructure only when it defines rules, versions, and submission practices
that make results interpretable across a changing field. Architecture 2.0 needs
a similar instinct for design loops. It must define the evaluation contract, preserve
provenance, track versions, and treat benchmark changes as part of the evidence
story.

## Security, IP, and Confidentiality Boundaries

Calibration, provenance, and benchmark governance make a claim assessable, but
they do not support commitment if the evidence cannot be audited by the relevant
rejection authority. Confidentiality
is therefore part of the same assurance chain as
fidelity and provenance, not a separate compliance concern. Evidence that must
stay private still has to be auditable enough to grade the claim and to reject
it. Otherwise, a loop can hide an unsupportable result behind a claim of
confidentiality. Architecture state is
often sensitive. RTL, design specifications, process assumptions, timing
constraints, floorplans, tool logs, customer workloads, proprietary traces,
compiler settings, design reviews, and deployment telemetry can all reveal
valuable or restricted information.

This need for auditable privacy has a direct technical consequence. Security boundaries are part of the
environment and evidence design. A loop must define what data can leave an
organization, what must remain local, what can be summarized, which artifacts
can be shared publicly, which logs should be redacted, and which generative methods or
tools can access each class of information. The assurance question is not only
whether the method is accurate. It is whether the loop preserves the
constraints under which architecture work actually happens.
The failure mode is not only leakage; it is a generative method producing a claim from
evidence it cannot expose, audit, or legally reuse.
For generated designs, the boundary also includes license provenance, ownership
of generated artifacts, export or release classification, disclosure authority,
and the reviewer role allowed to inspect redacted evidence. These are commitment
boundaries for the loop, not a substitute for legal review.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="The Policy Boundary Check"}
Can the AI model's evidence be audited without violating security, IP, license,
export-control, or disclosure boundaries? If a generative method produces a
claim using confidential or restricted data that cannot be exposed to the
rejection path, the claim cannot support commitment. The card must name which AI
tools can access each data class, how private evidence can be verified, and
which roles hold redaction, compliance-review, waiver, and release rights.
:::

This requirement also defines the bounds of microarchitectural security. A loop
that optimizes IPC without an isolation constraint can
behave like an accidental adversary. It may propose structures that improve
visible performance by sharing predictors, translation lookaside buffers (TLBs),
caches, or buffers across trust boundaries, while the proxy leaves side-channel
risk unpriced. Architecture 2.0 requires an early isolation screen (@lst-side-channel), a
cheap structural check that a generated layout does not silently share state
across trust boundaries. It is necessary but not sufficient, since it misses
contention, transient-execution, and simultaneous multithreading (SMT) channels.
A higher-fidelity security gate must still include formal non-interference (a guarantee that a component's
secret state cannot affect what an untrusted observer sees) [@GoguenMeseguer1982] or side-channel verification where the commitment requires it. A complete security
screen also covers memory-integrity and disturbance faults, not only
information-flow side channels. A design that trims memory-integrity margins to
meet a power budget can make read disturbance a rejection gate in its own right.

::: {.callout-field-note title="When the missing gate shipped: Spectre"}
For decades, improving instructions per cycle has been a powerful
microarchitectural optimization pressure, pursued through increasingly
aggressive speculation and reuse of predictors, caches, and buffers across
execution contexts. Ordinary functional tests could pass because speculation
computed the correct architectural results. In 2018 that same optimization
pressure was shown to have built a class of side channels that leak across
privilege boundaries [@KocherEtAl2019Spectre]. Ordinary functional checks did
not catch it because the objective the loop could see, IPC, and the leakage it
could not, were different quantities.

**Lesson:** Spectre is a reminder that functional correctness is not the same as security. If non-interference and timing-channel evidence sit outside the loop, performance wins can accumulate risk that ordinary correctness tests never see.
:::

```{.python #lst-side-channel lst-cap="**Security Gate Check:** An illustrative Python snippet rejecting RTL candidates that share state across trust boundaries."}
def check_side_channel_interference(rtl_candidate, isolation_policy):
    """
    Rung 1 Security Gate: Rejects RTL that shares speculative structures
    across defined security domains before physical synthesis.
    """
    shared_structures = find_shared_state(
        rtl_candidate, type=["BPU", "Cache", "TLB"]
    )
    for struct in shared_structures:
        if crosses_domain(struct, isolation_policy):
            return Reject(
                reason=(f"Security violation: {struct.name} shares "
                        "state across trust boundaries."),
                action="Partition the structure or flush on context switch."
            )
    return EscalateToSecurityVerification()
```

Codified as the explicit screen in @lst-side-channel, this check catches the most obvious performance-for-isolation trades early. Passing the check permits the candidate to advance to the next security-verification rung; it does not authorize the design itself. A surviving candidate must still escalate to formal non-interference or side-channel verification (for example, information-flow tracking) before commitment.

For shared community infrastructure, this means the field should distinguish between
public artifacts and private state. Public benchmarks, datasets, papers, and
gym environments can help bootstrap shared progress. Private workloads,
proprietary RTL, product traces, and process-specific assumptions often cannot
be released. Architecture 2.0 should support both. The design-loop card,
environment contract, and supporting evidence record should let a project describe
what kind of evidence exists without forcing disclosure of sensitive material.
That record should include a data-access and evidence-scope field to specify what private
state was used, what public proxy cannot support, which generative methods or tools may
access each artifact, and who owns disclosure exceptions.

The practical rule for AI-assisted systems is therefore simple. Do not make
confidentiality or policy invisible. If a claim depends on private, licensed, or
restricted data, say what class of data supports it, what auditing is possible,
what cannot be disclosed, who may inspect the redacted evidence, and what public
proxy would be insufficient. That is more honest than pretending every
architecture loop can be reproduced from public web data.

## Evidence Disputes and Decision Review {#sec-evidence-disputes-and-the-trust-checklist}

Even after accuracy and policy boundaries are stated, commitment must remain
reviewable when evidence is disputed. Such disputes are inevitable. One group
may claim that a learning-based method improves a design flow. Another may argue that the baseline, workload,
constraint set, tool version, or evaluation protocol was incomplete. A company
may have private evidence that cannot be released. A paper may report a strong
result but omit failed runs and rejected alternatives. A benchmark may reward behavior that matters
less in deployment. These disputes should not be treated as distractions from
Architecture 2.0. They are part of the field learning how to adjudicate claims.

While the specifics vary, the anatomy of an evidence dispute is stable. It
includes the claim, supporting evidence, counterevidence and failed checks,
fidelity, assumptions, coverage, provenance, rejection path, waiver, and
authorized decision.

Tracing how a contested claim is evaluated makes that anatomy visible (@fig-evidence-dispute-anatomy). A dispute is not
resolved by the system's confidence. The evidence packet makes the claim
reviewable, while a named role applies the declared rules and owns the resulting
acceptance, rejection, or escalation.

![**Evidence disputes become tractable when support and authority are separate:** A contested architecture claim should expose its supporting evidence, fidelity and scope, provenance, baseline, counterevidence, and failed checks. A named review role, not the evidence packet itself, owns acceptance, rejection, or escalation under the declared rules.](images/F9b-evidence-dispute-anatomy){#fig-evidence-dispute-anatomy width="100%" fig-alt="Flow diagram separating a contested architecture claim's evidence packet from the named review role authorized to accept, reject, or escalate it."}

The macro-placement dispute from the war story above returns here as a full
evidence-dispute anatomy. Independent groups disputed the 2021 claim
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement] on exactly the axes above, the baselines,
the released code, and the reproducibility of the protocol
[@ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL]. The point here is
not to adjudicate that dispute. The disagreement, still open as of July 2026,
was never about whether the model ran; it was about provenance, baselines, and
what evidence could reject the result. One constructive response
has been to build reproducible, end-to-end benchmarks that score placement by
final power, performance, and area rather than an intermediate proxy
[@WangEtAl2025ChiPBench]. That is the kind of infrastructure the anatomy asks for.
A contested claim becomes more tractable once the loop's evidence, baselines, and
rejection authority are made explicit.
For Architecture 2.0, the reusable lesson is not the controversy itself; it is
that a loop claiming placement quality must expose represented placement state,
legal move/action space, workload and PPA records, baseline records, rejected
alternatives, and the independent authority that can block commitment. For
placement-style AI-assisted EDA, reproducibility often depends on exact
toolchain versions, environment constraints, seeds, legal action spaces, and
baseline records, not just model weights.
@sec-running-the-loop completes this move, filling all twelve card fields
retrospectively from the same public record and showing that the dispute broke
out exactly in the fields the record left empty.

To make this dispute anatomy practical, @tbl-trust-checklist turns the same
claim-to-decision chain into a compact review. It is
intended for reading papers, reviewing internal tools, evaluating student
projects, and deciding whether an AI-assisted loop is ready for a more
expensive commitment.

| **Question** | **What a credible answer contains** | **Warning sign** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **What output is under review?** | Candidate, ranking, report, code/RTL change, or commitment recommendation, with generator and method role named. | The result is described only as an AI improvement. |
| **What is the claim?** | A bounded architecture task, objective, workload, and commitment level. | Vague claims of automation or improvement. |
| **What loop state and action contract are exposed?** | State fields, legal actions, tool or environment APIs, invalid transitions, and runnable receipts. | The loop can act through hidden state or unlogged tool semantics. |
| **What evidence supports it?** | Claim-relevant metrics, tool outputs, logs, and reviews with their available evaluation resources. | Only the winning score is shown. |
| **What weakens or blocks it?** | Counterevidence, failed runs, rejected alternatives, coverage gaps, warnings, and unresolved disagreements. | Contrary results or failed checks are omitted. |
| **What is the fidelity?** | Proxy, simulation, synthesis, emulation, signoff, deployment, or silicon level stated explicitly. | Treating all measurements as equivalent. |
| **What is the provenance?** | Workload and exact tool versions, tech nodes, configs, seeds, run-to-run variance or other nondeterminism, constraints, assumptions, and baselines needed for the declared audit or replay claim. | Hidden scripts, unstated defaults, or unrecorded nondeterminism. |
| **What is the security, IP, license, export-control, or disclosure boundary?** | Data classes, access rules, redaction limits, license or disclosure class, release or export-control review record, public proxy limits, authorized reviewer role, and an audit path. | Private or restricted evidence is treated as invisible or unverifiable. |
| **What can reject it?** | The rejection condition, executing check, independence basis, transition blocked, accountable owner, and waiver authority. | A check is named without the right or independence needed to block anything. |
| **Who may authorize commitment?** | A named role or process with the required decision right, accepted consequences, waiver scope, and reopening conditions. | The loop silently turns output into decision. |

: **Commitment review follows the decision chain, not tone:** A credible Architecture 2.0 claim exposes its supporting evidence, counterevidence, fidelity, provenance, policy boundary, rejection and waiver paths, and accountable decision rights before it advances. {#tbl-trust-checklist tbl-colwidths="[24,38,26]"}

This review is a readout of the design-loop card and its supporting records, not
a second schema. A paper, tool, or
project does not need to solve the whole lighthouse prompt to be valuable. It
does need to say where it sits in the loop, what evidence it produces, what it
cannot prove, what can block it, and which role may authorize the requested
commitment.

## Open Research Questions

The commitment-control mechanisms presented in this chapter establish a
foundation, but robust automation still needs concrete artifacts. The questions
here ask how evidence is bound, how proxy exploitation is detected, how
rejection authority is tested, how compromised evidence sources can be
stress-tested, and how confidential evidence can remain reviewable.

1. **How should supporting evidence records limit commitment?**
Fidelity ladders, commitment levels, and reversibility all assume that evidence
remains attached to the claim it supports. A fundamental systems challenge is
to design a record format and checker that bind proxy observations, simulation
runs, synthesis results, waivers, rejection events,
redactions, and reopening conditions into a unified audit object. Its value
would be measured by tamper detection, replayability, and whether the checker
prevents a candidate from exceeding the commitment level supported by its
evidence. The fidelity-ladder and commitment sections,
@sec-fidelity-ladders-and-evidence-ledgers and @sec-commitment-levels-and-reversibility, define the evidence boundary this
checker must enforce.

2. **What benchmarks expose proxy exploitation before commitment?**
Proxy mismatch, metric gaming, and calibration failures become more severe when
generators learn to exploit cheap architectural models. A thesis-level
contribution would build adversarial proxy benchmarks that include misleading
wins, support-boundary violations, and candidates that need stronger evidence.
The evaluation should report how often a loop escalates before accepting a
misleading candidate, plus the false-reject and evidence-cost tradeoffs of that
escalation. The motivation comes directly from @sec-proxy-mismatch-metric-gaming-and-calibration.

3. **How can rejection authorities be tested for independence?**
The rejection authority is only useful if it is not captured by the method,
proxy, dataset, or objective that produced the candidate. The research artifact
is an independence test for rejection authorities, using withheld workloads,
independently configured tools, human review protocols, or orthogonal checks.
The test should measure whether the authority catches failure modes that the
generating method cannot see and whether it resists being optimized around by
the loop.

4. **How should loops stress-test compromised evidence sources?**
The decision review in @sec-evidence-disputes-and-the-trust-checklist audits a
single claim, but the field also needs methodology for whole closed-loop
systems under pressure. A useful stress-test harness would inject hidden
workload drift, prompt injection, poisoned traces, compromised baselines,
tampered receipts, stale tool versions, side-channel-sensitive metrics, or
deceptive proxies into the design process.
The report should state false commitment rates, escalation cost, and whether the
accountable rejection path remains effective when the loop's evidence sources
become unreliable.

5. **How can confidential evidence remain auditable?**
Architecture evidence often depends on proprietary workloads, physical design
flows, licensed reports, PDK (Process Design Kit)-bound evidence, internal baselines, or deployment
data that cannot be shared directly.
The contribution is an audit protocol that exposes fidelity, provenance,
rejection, and commitment boundaries while redacting protected design state. It
should state what a reviewer can verify, what remains private, who is authorized
to inspect the redacted evidence, and what kinds of claims are too dependent on
confidential or restricted evidence to support public commitment.

## Conclusion

This chapter asked how feedback becomes scoped evidence and how that evidence
may support an authorized commitment. The answer is one visible chain. The
claim names its scope. Supporting evidence records what was measured and under
which assumptions. Counterevidence, failed checks, and rejected alternatives
remain attached. The rejection path exposes what it can block, how it is
independent, who owns it, and who may waive it. The final decision names the
right being exercised and the consequences being accepted.

Architects must understand this chain even when tools execute routine gates.
Architecture work sets the claim scope, determines what may be delegated,
judges evidence sufficiency, assigns waiver rights, and accepts cross-system
consequences. Those rights may be distributed across several accountable roles,
but they cannot disappear into model confidence or tool automation.

The durable move is to escalate with commitment. Cheap feedback may screen
candidates, and stronger observations may support a higher commitment, but only
when the chain records uncertainty, counterevidence, rejection and waiver
rights, and accountable approval. A proxy win that cannot name what would
overturn it or which transition can be blocked has not met this bar.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** Advance a claim only through a visible chain of supporting evidence, counterevidence, failed checks, rejection or waiver, and accountable decision rights.
- **Reader test:** What would overturn the AI-assisted claim, which transition would that check block, who owns the consequence, and who may waive the failure?
- **Up next:** @sec-running-the-loop applies this commitment chain inside a complete design loop and inspects the records it leaves behind.
:::
